altText.1: Question 1. What does the word “quest” mean as used in paragraph 4? 1 A difficult assignment B determined search C favorite activity D careful planning
altText.2: Question 2. Which sentence best states a theme of paragraph 7? A __ Giving someone a gift is just as much fun as receiving a gift. B Buying a gift from a store is easier than making a gift. C Cards with messages inside create wonderful memories. D There are many ways to show someone that you care.
altText.3: Question 3. How does paragraph 14 relate to paragraphs 19 through 22? 3 A Paragraph 14 explains the setting in paragraphs 19 through 22. Paragraph 14 describes the cause for the events in paragraphs 19 B through 22. Paragraph 14 introduces a character developed in paragraphs 19 C through 22. D Paragraph 14 gives a problem that is solved in paragraphs 19 through 22.
altText.4: Question 4. In paragraph 18, why does Daisy ask Miss Goldner to try saying the word? 4 A She wants Miss Goldner to pronounce the word for the class. B She wants to make sure Miss Goldner heard the word correctly. C She wants everyone to see how much Miss Goldner loves the word. D She wants Miss Goldner to see how fun the word is to say and hear.
altText.5: Question 5. Which detail best shows how Samantha changes from the beginning to the end of the story? A Samantha walks home with Daisy and Emma. B Samantha says that Daisy brought the best gift. C = Samantha worries that Mrs. Joseph will be upset. D Samantha thinks of different words to describe the day.
altText.6: Question 6. What point is the author making in the story? 6 A Words can be powerful. B Learning can be fun. C Learning is easier with friends. D Words should be spoken loudly.
altText.19: Question 19. What is the meaning of the word “breakthrough” as it is used in paragraph 2 of the 19 article? A funny story B strange event C difficult challenge D important change
altText.20: Question 20. What is a central idea of paragraph 4? 20 A David Greybeard had fun looking through Goodall’s campsite. B Goodall’s actions helped her build trust with David Greybeard. C David Greybeard spent most of each day looking for and eating food. D Goodall was so still and quiet that David Greybeard was unaware of her.
altText.21: Question 21. How does paragraph 5 support a central idea of the article? 21 By explaining that Goodall’s work became easier over time, it shows that A repeating her tasks eventually made her an expert. By telling that she was able to come close to the chimps, it shows that B Goodall’s thoughtful and patient study of the animals was a success. By demonstrating how Goodall was able to become friendly with David C Greybeard, it shows that Goodall learned how to communicate with chimps. By detailing the different steps in the process of her work, it shows that D Goodall did the same work as other scientists but got better results.
altText.22: Question 22. Which detail from the article shows how Goodall affected future researchers? 22 “According to Goodall’s camp cook, the chimpanzee had spent about an A hour eating fruit from one of the oil palms that grew nearby.” (paragraph 1) “It was the summer of 1961. For a year, Goodall had been studying the B chimps living in Gombe Stream Reserve near Lake Tanganyika in Central Africa.” (paragraph 2) “Goodall’s career began as a childhood wish to see African animals in their C natural environments.” (paragraph 6) “She also opened the door for other women to become ethnologists, D scientists who study animal behavior.” (paragraph 6)
altText.23: Question 23. How does the structure of the article help the reader understand the events? 23 By explaining the sequence of events over time, it shows how each small A step was important to Goodall’s work. By comparing Goodall’s thoughts about her work at different times, it B shows how she changed her research over time. By providing a description of the camp, it shows why Goodall’s work was C special and important. By listing the problems and solutions of studying chimpanzees, it shows D why Goodall’s work changed ideas in science.
altText.26: Question 26. How does the illustration support the information in paragraph 2? 26 A It shows parts of the bird that are usually eaten. B It shows why the furcula has an unusual name. C It shows why all birds have wishbones. D It shows where the furcula is on the body.
altText.27: Question 27. Read this sentence from paragraph 1. But one of the most popular—pulling the wishbone—dates back centuries. Which sentence from the article best supports this claim? A “The winner is the person who ends up with the bigger piece” (paragraph 3) “The practice of making a wish on a bird’s furcula dates back to the ancient B___Etruscans, a people who lived in what is today northern Italy.” (paragraph 4) Cc “Passersby would pick it up, stroke it for good luck, and make a wish” (paragraph 4) “Today a wishbone tug-of-war is as much a part of Thanksgiving as is gobbling down turkey smothered in gravy’ (paragraph 6)
altText.28: Question 28. How are the details in paragraphs 4 through 6 organized? 28 A by presenting descriptions of some points B by presenting a problem and a solution C by comparing and contrasting events D by listing events in the order they happened
altText.29: Question 29. How does the information in the section “Through the Ages” support a central idea of the article? A __ Itshows that today turkey and gravy are eaten for Thanksgiving. B__It shows that some people believed birds had unusual powers. C It shows that there were many wild turkeys in the New World. D _ Itshows that many people share the history of the wishbone.
altText.30: Question 30. Which sentence best explains how to win at tug-of-war with a turkey wishbone? “At Thanksgiving, the tradition is for two people to hold one end of the bone, make a wish, and then tug” (paragraph 3) B “But there are a few tips that may help you get the upper hand.” (paragraph 7) Cc “The more pressure you can apply to your side of the wishbone, the better chance you have . .” (paragraph 8) D “Whether you end up with the larger or the smaller piece of the furcula is ultimately a matter of luck” (paragraph 9)
altText.31: Question 31. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A _ Breaking a wishbone is just one of many Thanksgiving traditions. B All birds have a wishbone that helps to support the birds as they fly. Cc Making wishes with a wishbone is a tradition that began thousands of years ago. D There was once a shortage of chickens in Rome because the wishbone tradition was so popular.
